text: We present a standardized single-dog, totally robotic technique for total mesorectal excision.
After a short summary of docking, port placement, and colonic mobilization, the video will focus on the technical aspects of the TME.
At the end, we present our results of our clinical experience at Portsmouth.
Preoperative docking simulation is critical to confirm exact docking position of the patient's side cart. This helps to make actual docking quick, as minimal adjustments are needed.
After establishing pneumoperitoneum using an open technique, the surgeon inserts ports sequentially under direct vision, following the positions indicated in the diagram.
The patient's side cart is docked to the patient's left hip, aligning with the preoperative simulation setup.
The robotic arms are attached to the ports, beginning with the camera arm, and their positioning and freedom of movement are checked.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral approach by incising the peritoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is identified, and the dissection plane is maintained close to the posterior aspect of the IMA. The dissection progresses until the left ureter and gonadal vessels are clearly visible.
The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), removing surrounding fatty tissue. Hemologs are applied to secure the IMA proximally and distally before it is divided.
The surgeon continues to develop the medial to lateral dissection plane, carefully avoiding penetration through Toll's fascia, while maintaining proximity to the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon follows the inferior mesenteric vein to the duodenal-jejunal junction and divides it after applying hemolog clips.
The surgeon develops the medial plane further, breaking into the lesser sac just above the lower border of the pancreas. The lesser sac is clearly visualized from the medial aspect.
A swab is placed at the top of the pancreas to aid in its identification during the procedure.
The surgeon releases the colon laterally, connecting it with the previously mobilized medial plane, ensuring the colon is free from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon disengages R-II and uses R-I with monopolar scissors and R-III with bipolar forceps to divide the gastrocolic ligament, while the assistant provides downward counter-traction. Upon entering the lesser sac, the swab placed over the pancreas is visualized and retrieved. The mobilization of the splenic flexure is completed by releasing the lateral attachments of the splenocolic ligament.

An additional eight millimeter port is inserted in the left iliac fossa for the total mesorectal excision (TME). The pro grasp instrument, labeled R-II, is attached to the newly inserted port in the left iliac fossa.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) by developing the posterior plane using articulating monopolar scissors in R-I for precise dissection. The surgeon's left hand provides counter-traction with R-III and attached bipolar forceps, extending the dissection distally.
The surgeon carefully dissects anterior to the presacral fascia, ensuring the preservation of the hypogastric nerves and avoiding the presacral venous plexus.

The surgeon develops the interior plane by making a peritoneal cut two centimeters above the peritoneal reflection, starting from the right side. The high-definition three-dimensional view allows for precise dissection, preserving the fine nerve fibers located posterior to the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon dissects the lateral pillars, joining them to the anterior plane from the left side. The completed posterior dissection aids in this step, while care is taken to avoid dissecting too laterally.
The surgeon carefully develops the anterior plane anterior to the adenovillous fascia, utilizing traction and counter-traction techniques to achieve high precision in the dissection.

The surgeon uses traction with R-II and counter-traction with R-III to develop the left and right sides of the surgical field down to the pelvic floor, ensuring precise manipulation of the tissue planes.
The surgeon carefully navigates the dissection to avoid going too wide, where nerves may be tented in, while joining the posterior and anterior planes in the total mesorectal excision (TME).

The surgeon carefully divides the denim villi's fascia anteriorly to thin out the rectal tube, ensuring no diathermy damage occurs. The dissection progresses to reach the pelvic floor, exposing the overlying endopelvic fascia.

The surgeon changes the right iliac fossa port to a twelve millimeter port to accommodate the laparoscopic stapler. A roticulating forty-five millimeter stapler is used to perform an anterior-posterior transection, with two fire rings visible during the stapling process.
The surgeon maneuvers the short-length stapler within the narrow pelvis, achieving accurate positioning for the transection.
The surgeon inspects the pelvis, clearly identifying the intact left and right hypogastric nerves. The smooth and intact total mesorectal excision (TME) package is demonstrated.

Following the resection of the rectum, the surgeon brings out the spike of the circular stapler at the junction of the two staple lines. The anastomosis is performed, and a flexible sigmoidoscopy is used to visually check the integrity of the anastomosis.
During our initial clinical experience over a seven-month period, We performed twenty robotic TMEs for rectal cancer.
Sixty-five percent of these were male, ten were for mid to low rectal cancers, twenty percent had neoadjuvant radiotherapy, and about a third were obese.

Our median operating time was two hundred and thirty-one minutes, with a median length of stay of seven days. No conversions were required, and no cases developed an anastomotic leak TME is a technically challenging operation both by open and laparoscopic approaches.
We hypothesize that robotic TME has potential benefits in high-risk rectal cancer patients.
